author: @lnwolf41
text: They do not understand the scale of the experiment.  Will it power a car? no, will it provide power to run a factory ? probably not.  Could it charge batteries that light a roadway at night? yes. So lets say his dish is 2 square inches, produces 3 volts at 0.002 amps to produce  0.006 watts.  now lets create a 100 square yard device each at 2sq inches = 12,960,000 sq inches. produces a total of 38,880 volts,  25,920 amps at 77,760 watts of power.  even if we produce a third of the power that's still 25,920 watts, this is still a usable amount of power. Use it to charge several iron flow batteries, these last 20 years and are non-toxic.

author: @dougsteere6814
text: As I have been  watching off and on I would like to know what conducting inks you can heat up your house as we are buying a different one and would like to paint the floor or walls in the new house to heat it up in stead of gas could you please help us as I  retired an want to save money THANK YOU VERY MUCH

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Hiya Doug. The inks produce a resistive heater and all resistive heaters are around 98% efficient - that means it doesn't matter which kind of heater you use they are all pretty much the same in terms of creating heat output from electricity input. You won't save money by changing the heating element. The real saving comes from the ability to only heat the zone you are living in. The standard way to heat a room is by forced air if you are using HVAC or wet radiators if you are using that system. They need to heat the entire room top down with the air at the cieling getting warm first. With underfloor heating you heat the room bottom up and as you basically sit in or walk around the bottom part of your room you 'feel' the heat more quickly - this lets you use less energy as you feel warmer - that's where the saving is. So what I am saying here is the heating element doesn't matter much - how you heat your home is far more important to saving money

replies.author: @RWBHere
replies.text: A properly-designed heat pump can be well over 100% efficient, because it works by effectively 'throwing heat away' from the volume which is being cooled, into the volume which becomes warmed. So the radiator of a heat exchanger, buried under the floor, and whose cold volume is outdoors, should be the lowest-cost solution over the life of the system. Your refrigerator is a simple heat pump, in which heat is extracted from inside the appliance, and 'thrown away' via the radiator at the rear of the machine.

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
For me, its not even a question of if it works, of course it works, but rather, if it is practical. The plant part is as easy as planting a lawn over the substrate [although people walking on it could be a bit of a disaster] and the soil is as cheap as dirt, but are the special chemicals and substrates affordable on a scaled up basis so that individuals or companies can afford to install acres and acres of it?

If the basic materials needed to make it work can be turned out in [for instance] 3 meter by 100 meter rolls, then one can determine the cost of mass production, and begin to see just how useful it is on a grand scale, seeing as there are many places where people never walk [and hopefully also no large, heavy, hooved animals] that would serve as ideal year-round solar power collectors without a single soul even knowing there were anything different about the place.

Certain select parks, botanical gardens, estates of wealthy people, roofs of high rise and warehouse buildings and the like would make very obvious natural locations for literally billions of square meters of this technology, so long as it is not physically harmed by anything 'walking' or in any way deforming it so that the grids get damaged.

replies.author: @StefanHartmann-hartiberlin
replies.text: You can just use 2 layers of coconut charcoal, which is pretty conductive and grow plants between it and use the coconut charcoal layers as the 2 electrodes, so yes, it is very cheap...this felt is only needed, if you want to have your roots of the plants between it, but the carbon felt is pretty expensive... but coconut husk charcoal should also work...and you can put it ontop of the dirt and only have a 1 to 2 cm layer, so the charcoal makes contact with each other...so each charcoal layer must be layed this way, that it does not shortout the 2 layers, but only makes contact to itsself...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: there is quite a a lot of research on this -  there are certainly companies trying to bring this to market - so - i personally have no idea - but other folks seem to think it's awesome enough and cheap enough to try and do it mass scale

replies.author: @DeliciousDeBlair
replies.text:
@StefanHartmann-hartiberlin This is where experimentation and exploring new ideas will really pay off for the future of such a project. Any kind of affordable mat one can devise, which will last for many years without breaking down, and also be pliable enough to be installed without disintegrating should be looked at.

Don't forget that it can not be brittle, so it must have enough flexibility to be handled, including being grabbed by one edge and moved, lifted, lowered, buried under soil and other mechanical stresses, and to top that off, the very electrolytic affect of plant ions which break down soil into its atomic components to absorb them will likely attack the substrate just like how graphite electrodes will disintegrate while being used in electrolytic chemical refinement.

replies.author: @StefanHartmann-hartiberlin
replies.text: @DeliciousDeBlair You can probably make some kind of graphitic foam yourself... maybe also by just painting Rob´s ink onto cotton wool and then use an oven or kiln to heat it up without oxygen  and generate something simular like a graphitic felt matt this way..Also you could just hammer or press the coconut husk charcoal into graphoil plates yourself as it is simularly done to generate these graphfoil plates...
